title: Hyper-V VM with User Added Network Adapters
body:
### Feature request description

Make adding a Network Adapter to the Hyper-V VM work. Currently the machine will not start properly if the Network Adapter is added before the machine is started. Adding a network adapter is useful for providing direct connectivity to the Local Area Network and makes podman networks like macvlan/ipvlan work better.

### Suggest potential solution

Making Network Adapters added to the Hyper-V VM before startup work.
OR
Provide a workflow during the machine creation for adding a Network Adapter.

If the first can be done the user is then free to add as many adapters as they need and configure them as needed.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

If the Network Adapter is added after the machine is started it works. This is annoying to have to remove and re-add the network adapter every time the machine is started.

### Additional context

Something right now seems to depend on there being no other interface in the VM it probably causes vsock0 to not get properly configured. Even a Network Adapter added but not connected (the "Virtual switch:" option for the added Network Adapter set to "Not connected") seems to break things. What happens is that the `podman machine start` command fails with the below message and subsequent podman commands (other than shutdown or rm) fail to connect.

> Error: ssh: handshake failed: read tcp 127.0.0.1:36355->127.0.0.1:35438: wsarecv: An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host.

VM's network interfaces (from the Hyper-V VM Manager GUI as `podman machine ssh` won't work) note that I have specified a virtual switch for the VM to use in this case, but it shouldn't matter. It seems that the `vsock0` interface in the VM isn't connected.

```
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    REMOVED FOR BREVITY
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc mq state UP group default qlen 1000
    REDACTED
5: vsock0: <NO-CARRIER,BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state DOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/ether REDACTED brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet6 REDACTED/64 scope link tentative noprefixroute
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
```

From discussion here https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1557#issuecomment-1716137519 a vsock interface seems to be required to make the typical installation require no complex steps to get working. This however limits the networking capabilities of the guest VM to be perhaps worse than WSL2 with mirrored networking (possibly even without). Namely getting access to the hosts networks directly

comments.body: We do not do this for other machine providers and it really is outside the spirit of podman machine.  that said, lets see what interest is garnered here.

This does work via WSL to some extent with the newish mirrored networking configuration. However the lacking thing with WSL is there's no way to do macvlan networks and I also had some trouble with ipvlan networks. Which makes seeking other alternatives (creating your own VM) required. This also means you have to connect to the VM through the Hyper-V VM view or setup SSH access which just makes using it clunky. If podman supported this the users avoid the headache of installing and using such workarounds.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
